interest: Teacher-side actors (union and teacher advocate) whose members would bear the burden of structural reform without the human-capital base to deliver it, joined by employers who want reliable credential signals that degraded comprehensive staffing would erode.
value: Feasibility and equity-in-delivery are in tension with the abstract equity ambition of S3: a reform that cannot be staffed will harm the very equity it promises.
fear: That comprehensive upper grades will be staffed by under-qualified, overworked or burnt-out teachers, concentrating shortage effects on the reform and harming equity more than current early selection, with teachers bearing retraining costs for a reform that may be reversed.
assumption: It assumes teacher supply cannot be stabilised on the reform's timeline and that the shortage is a binding, non-negotiable precondition S3 does not resolve rather than a parameter that could be addressed by wage/retention measures before scale-up.
empirical_uncertainty: The teacher-shortage constraint is documented as 'strong'/binding with 'low' confidence on stabilisation, so the factual core (shortage exists) is settled but whether it can be stabilised in time is contested/unknown and pivotal to the argument.
